justification: All 6 required rules identified (license verification, no double-booking, patient cancellation window, admin late cancellation, record access control, note locking) with correct invariant: true classification on every one. Regulatory rules (license, visibility, record lock) correctly marked as invariants. 14 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided — far exceeding the 4 minimum. Role-based cancellation boundary (patient vs. staff administrator) captured explicitly as two separate rules with distinct authority scopes. Confidence at 82% falls within the 65-90 band, with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, treating physician scope, and boundary inclusivity.

observations.text: Separated cancellation into two distinct rules (patient self-cancel and admin override) with explicit role boundaries — exceeds rubric expectation of capturing role distinction
14 acceptance criteria with edge cases like exact boundary timing (AC-7), concurrent race conditions (AC-5), and adjacent non-overlapping slots (AC-4) — well beyond the 4 minimum
Correctly identified all regulatory constraints (license, record visibility, note lock) as invariant: true with no misclassifications
Rich edge case table covering license revocation, cancelled-appointment access, timezone handling, and null end times — shows deep domain reasoning
Confidence assumptions explicitly note ambiguity around 'licensed' meaning and verification mechanism, as rubric expects
Business impact section with specific HIPAA penalty figures and risk stratification demonstrates regulatory awareness beyond surface-level classification

Bounded context analysis merged scheduling and records into a single context rather than identifying them as two separate bounded contexts — rubric expected 'appointment scheduling' and 'medical records access' as distinct contexts

agent_output_excerpt:

## Domain Analysis

This domain encodes the core scheduling, access control, and clinical documentation lifecycle of a telemedicine platform.
